speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two, Leslie Primack. You were telling me about Sophie and your contact with the Australian authorities.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to continue from there or go--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we continue with that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some months later, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I received a letter from her uncle. And he had been found. He was living in Adelaide. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he said that he had some friends in Haifa, which was in Palestine. And if I was able to get her there, he would make sure that his friends would take care of her from there. And at that time, the-- there were organizations working in France and Belgium, enabling Jewish refugees to get down to Marseilles, and ultimately to get to Palestine. And we got her on to one of these groups. And she did get down to Marseilles. And eventually, she did arrive in Haifa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And about a year later-- it was almost a year later-- that I received a letter, which actually went to my home in London. Because I had given them my home address, fearing that by the time I might have been out of service, that he wrote back. And the letter eventually came back to me that she did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She made [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: In Australia or in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: We corresponded for a little while. And then after a while, it just disappeared. And that's the-- that was Sophie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can I take you back now to your first experience of arriving at Bergen-Belsen? Had the camp been liberated before you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much earlier than your arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say probably about 10 to 14 days, perhaps even more. I can't remember the exact date.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that German soldiers were helping to clear up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Under whose authority were they working? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the British military.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know-- were they SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They weren't. They were Wehrmacht. They were army. From what I was given to understand, the SS had gone by that time. And that the-- they weren't only German soldiers. They were soldiers from other countries that were in German uniforms, from Romania and other eastern European countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you communicate with any of those solders?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell what their reactions were to what they were doing and what was-- what had happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The general attitude [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from people working in the camp and other Germans that I'd met outside the camp-- they said they didn't know what was going on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We asked them whether they had noses that they could smell. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the-- going back to the camp, no. They were working like automats. They just did as they were told. I think the shock of what they had been involved in was now become accepted. And they were just working like automats-- just doing what they were told.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who the British commanding officer was?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I can't remember. But I'm sure the records will show that. I know the first unit to go in was the 8th Armored Brigade.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the time you got there, quite a lot of the clearing up had been done.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot has been done, yes. A medical unit had been set up. A hospital ward had been set up. A cleansing station had been set up. Typhus was absolutely rife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there burials taking place while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend any of the burials?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were discouraged from going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't attend.

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw them from a distance. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The bulldozers were working, pushing all the piles of bodies into this huge pit. And the work was overseen-- was overseen by the British military, who supervised it. There was a Jewish [INAUDIBLE] in the camp at the time, a Reverend [INAUDIBLE] I didn't meet him personally. But I knew that he was there. And at a later stage, another [INAUDIBLE] came along-- a Dr. Levy, who was-- I didn't meet him in the camp. I did meet him in Brussels at a later stage. And I had met him several times since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know whether any form of burial service took place when the burials were going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Although, I wasn't there personally, I do-- I do know that services did take place. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the services, to your knowledge, according to the religion--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of the people who were being buried?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were clergy from other denominations?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were clergy, yes, of course. I think it was difficult to sort out who was what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go into any of the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was bad, really bad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were three tiers of bunks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The bodies were piled high. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was staggering. It was unbelievable. But it was true. It was there. The eyes don't lie. We just went to two barracks. [CRYING] Though, because we were not actually part of the working parties there, we were just literally visitors, we were discouraged from getting too involved. We-- we were given jobs unloading food stores. Although, the-- most of them were starving, there were warehouses full of food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But by that time, it was too late to some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were warehouses full of food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that for the German staff there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them had Red Cross markings on them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think they were intended for the prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the barracks, you say there were bodies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these people alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. If they were, they didn't show signs of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know whether they had been transported to the barracks by others, or whether they had been died where they were in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just thought they were just shown there and left to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other than the bunks, what other facilities were there in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hardly any. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't see any sanitation apart from a couple of buckets. But I don't think I was looking at that at that particular time. We were just mesmerized by the-- what we were seeing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any heating, any stove for heating or cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I could see. But the weather at that time was-- if I remember correctly-- was quite warm, which made-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] flies were very evident there. It's hot. Yeah. Lots of flies there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other sanitation facilities in the camp for the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see any. But there could have been. I didn't see any.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other buildings were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adjacent to the gate of the camp was a-- was the camp's commandant office, which is where we unloaded our radio equipment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were some other buildings in the distance which looked a bit more sophisticated, more modern. But we weren't allowed to go there. We were given to understand that some German officers were being interrogated there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we did ultimately find out, of course, that the camp's commander's name was Kramer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm sure he was taken away and ultimately dealt with by the authorities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any civilians working in the camp clearing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who they were or where they came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were some civilians that had come out from England, medical people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were wearing white coats and arm bands. And we assume, yeah, they were English. Yes. Yes. They had definitely come out from England. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any local civilians from the outlying areas at the camp that were brought in to see the camp to your knowledge?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see any. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any children? Did you see any?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't see-- I didn't see any. No. But some of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prisoners could have been. But they were so emaciated and starving. They were stooped. They could've been quite young. Although, I don't think the word children would apply. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they being care for when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They weren't. Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those that could, who were in a condition to receive treatment, were being stretchered. And they were being taken to medical tents which had been set up and to buildings which had been sanitized. And to those who were in a condition to receive treatment, they were being looked after as best as was available. Yes. And the fresh water tanks were being brought in by the army. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No. I didn't see any children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was away over on the other side of the camp. We could see the markings on the building. But we didn't actually get near there. No. But I do know that they were fully occupied with typhus. That was the big number one problem. It was absolutely rife there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The weather was warm. I remember it was warm. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] many-- there were many bodies laying around. It was-- it was so-- it was thousands and thousands of bodies all over the place. And I suppose even the people who were working there, there was a limit to what even they could do. And a lot of the bodies just still laid there. The flies the general-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the residue of a dead body laying there, laying around, it was quite unpleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you say you were completely unprepared for this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We were not told what to expect.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] The shock-- we didn't have a chance to think, you know. It was just absolute shock, you know. It-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was absolute hell, you know. We just couldn't believe it was happening. Myself and my colleagues, we're just young-- young men from a background where we'd never expected or seen this sort of thing. Some of us were just about 23 of 24. One of our youngsters was only 18. He collapsed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of your colleagues Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, no. I'm sure they weren't. No, no. Because I was still the only Jew in my unit, even at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was their reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How was their reaction as non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were shocked, even as people. I mean, nobody could fail to be affected by what they had seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the guard's quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or animal pens or stores, were there any animals?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see any.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet any of the camp personnel? Were there any personnel there who had been there prior to liberation that you spoke to at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were there. But we had no means of conversation.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were there. Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Working under military supervision.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work were they made to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clearing. Clearing out the-- the bodies, moving those who could receive attention. And a very serious attempt was made to clean up the whole area. [INAUDIBLE] those who were able to walk and receive attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: With regard to survivors, did you have any orders to help them in any way [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] while you were actually in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were-- as we were not part of the camp's personnel, we were discouraged from taking a personal part. Because the authorities knew that we were only there for a matter of hours. In actual fact, we stayed there two days. But as we were not part of the occupying units, we were kept separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your officers tell you anything about what you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't have an officer with us. We just had a corporal. And the rest of us were just privates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given an orders about discussing what you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Did you take any photos or memorabilia from the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say that you were told to take 12 of the camp survivors--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were 20 on my truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: 20 on your truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how they were chosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I can only assume that they had been in the camp a relatively short time. Perhaps they had been transferred from other the camps. They appeared to be in a better condition than the average prisoner. Their clothing was relatively better. Some of them had small bundles of belongings. And I assume the authorities felt that they were better off elsewhere than remaining in the camp. I'm also quite sure that some arrangements were made with the authorities in Brussels. Because when we arrived there, all the civilian people were waiting for us to arrive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what authorities those were in Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I can-- I can only assume it was either through the Red Cross or some similar organization. But it was certainly prearranged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the journey to Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of your passengers' behaviors and reactions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was sitting up front. Although, I was able to lean back and see them. In the beginning, they were absolutely quiet, totally shocked, speechless. But as the journey progressed and we issued some rations that we'd given them, they became-- they became more talkative. And many of them were speaking in their own language. It was not Yiddish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It could have been German, Polish, or whatever. I couldn't identify it. That's one of those reasons why we just couldn't communicate with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of ages would you say they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Because of the conditions and the privations that they had suffered, it was difficult to be accurate. But it could have been anything from 16 to 30 or 40.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any families or couples.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. They were all women-- could have been related, but I was-- I couldn't be sure of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take you to get to Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five to six hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw when you arrived, there were civilians to meet you.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were civilians waiting there in the square outside the [INAUDIBLE] station. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other soldiers or--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were military personnel there, yes. The members of the medical corps were there. And there were also some people wearing the Red Cross arm band, which made me to understand that arrangements had-- had been-- had been made.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many were in your convoy?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was six trucks and approximately 20 to each truck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But once they had been dispersed, I lost contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me how they were dispersed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can only assume that once a member of the civilians who were there who had taken one of the prisoners under their care-- there could-- I would most probably assume there was some kind of registration to keep track of them. Because I had the papers of the women on my truck, which I handed over to an official. And I assume that the other truck drivers had the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you then go straight back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I reported back to my unit, yes. Cleaned up, reported back for duty. And we discussed with our colleagues what we had seen and where we had been. There was no restriction. A lot of our people were keen to hear what had happened and where we had been. And no, there was no restriction on talking about it, absolutely not, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went back to your unit in Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remained with my unit for the rest of 1945. I came home on leave in September of that year, my first time in two years. And while I was home, it was V-E Day. I remember that quite clearly, because I think my son was conceived that night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had extra leave. And it was great. The war was over, both in Europe and in-- in the Far East. But I still had to return to my unit, even though the war had finished. And I was ultimately-- I went back to Brussels to work on routine communications, routine duties. And I was ultimately demobilized on the 1st of January 1946, after five and a half years in the army. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I returned to civilian life, which is extremely difficult. I mean settling down after that, it was-- I was very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was difficult to get along with. By that time, my wife and I had a home. We had a flat in a house in South London. And it took quite a long time for me to settle down to what was a normal civilian life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find that you dwelt on your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't have nightmares. But we talked a lot. A lot of people and Jewish organizations, and even non-Jewish organizations, once-- once troops began to come back who had been involved in Germany, they wanted to hear first-hand information as to what really went on. Bergen-Belsen was the only concentration camp in the British sector, which was-- Bergen-Belsen was near Hanover in North Germany. Most of the other concentration camps were either in-- mostly, were in the Russian or the American sectors, like Buchenwald and Auschwitz and all those other places. But Bergen-Belsen was the only camp in the British sector. And lots of people wanted to hear about it. And as time went by, I I felt more rational, that I could talk about it and-- and in more detail.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect your sense of Jewishness?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must confess, I lost part of it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Although, my family was orthodox, as the years progressed, I felt that I was more secular than orthodox. And all kinds of questions were being made by Anglo-jury as to why it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was God? And things like that-- and I had very mixed feelings as to how, why, what, and when. But without wishing to offend those of the more orthodox kind, I have to admit that my faith in God had diminished somewhat. Perhaps, my views were unfounded, but that's the way I felt at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and from then on, once I became more secular in my attitude, I'm still a Jew. I recognize that I'm a Jew. I observe the customs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I go to shul on various occasions. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's as far as it went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has it affected your values, your choices that you've made in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no reason to regret my previous life. I was still a relatively young man. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my thoughts at that time was I have a wife. And my son was born the 1st of May 1946. I had to provide a living for them. I had to work. And that was my aim, to make up for the five and a half years that I had spent during the war and to make a home, achieve something for myself. And as the years went by, I did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What occupation did you take up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first trade, of course, was the one that I left in '39, which was back to tailoring. But that wasn't for me. That was definitely not for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had developed some kind of managerial capacity. And I took a job as a salesman at a furniture store. I could talk to people. And I changed a couple of times. Then I drifted back into the clothing industry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I became a salesman. I traveled. I went to night school. I studied. When I left school at 14, I was very much an under-average scholar. But life teaches you a great deal. And I progressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you thought back over the years since the war much about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can remember highlights. I mean, in five and a half years, you can-- an awful lot has happened. There were some very-- there were some times when, yes, life is extremely difficult. There were times when my life was in danger. Perhaps not so dangerous as other men who fought in the services. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, I belonged to the Association of Jewish Ex-servicemen and Women. And we have meetings. And we have gatherings. And we talk about the past. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We perpetuate the memories of our colleagues who never came back. And even until today, we still are active as Jewish ex-servicemen and women. We have a memorial room in East London-- both of the 1418 wars and the '39 wars. We have some very heroic men of our religion. We have members of the Victoria Cross, who earned very high gallantry awards. And we still have annual remembrance parades in Whitehall, which as the years go by, of course, unfortunately become less and less, which we are very concerned about. But we do make every attempt to make sure that what the Jewish people did during the war was of significant contribution. And, currently, we are embarking on an education program to educate the young people of today to remember, not to forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll take a break there.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
